_comment: Conversational ('dialogue') Listen data per edition date → signal number → language. publish.py injects this as the optional top-level signal.listen block (see LISTEN_DIALOGUE_SCHEMA.md). Absent entry → no signal.listen injected → unchanged feed. `key` is an R2 object key under the public base (https://pub-95a7558772874b48a645ad0c1604d784.r2.dev/<key>); a full https URL is also accepted. Injection is best-effort: an incomplete/malformed track is skipped, never breaking publish. 2026-06-25 signal 1 is the EN prototype (root-level key). Future: add `ja` and date-scoped keys audio/YYYY-MM-DD/signal-0N-dialogue-<lang>.mp3.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So there's real news on Iran this morning?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yeah. The U.S. and Iran reached an early peace deal.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: And U.N. nuclear inspectors are going to visit Iran's sites.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Inspectors actually going in — that's a real step?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It is. They'll check the nuclear material in person, not just take Iran's word for it.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Part of the deal is that the most dangerous uranium gets watered down, with inspectors watching.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Is Iran fully on board?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Mostly. They say full access to the damaged sites comes later, in a final deal.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: And the U.S. says it won't sign anything that puts its allies at risk.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So why would someone far from the region care?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: One big reason — the Strait of Hormuz reopens.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: That's the narrow stretch of sea where a huge share of the world's oil ships through.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So this could reach everyday fuel prices.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Right. Calmer waters there usually means steadier energy costs everywhere.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: A quieter region, and maybe a quieter day at the gas pump.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: That's the hope. It's early — but it's a real move.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What's the big political story today?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Colombia's election is settled. The left's candidate, Ivan Cepeda, conceded.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: So a hard-right populist, Abelardo de la Espriella, won the run-off.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Was it close?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Very. About two hundred fifty thousand votes.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I heard there were fraud claims?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yeah. President Petro alleged fraud and foreign interference, before and after.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: But the national registry reported high accuracy in the recent counts.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So the result stands?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It does. Cepeda accepted it this morning.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: There's a hard but important story out of Pakistan.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Police rescued a French woman and her five children.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: She says her husband held her captive for over twelve years.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Twelve years?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes — with years of domestic abuse, in the country's north-west.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: How did they get out?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: One of her sons slipped out and reached a police station.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: The husband has been arrested, and there's an investigation.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Is this part of a bigger pattern?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Sadly yes. Domestic violence is still a serious problem there.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Something wild happened in tech and politics.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Two AI giants poured money into one local primary.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Anthropic and OpenAI — about twenty-seven million dollars combined.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: On a single primary?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yeah, in New York's twelfth district, over a candidate named Alex Bores.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Why him?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: A pro-AI super PAC targeted him, which only made him more popular.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Then Anthropic-linked PACs spent millions to defend him.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: And the result?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: He narrowly lost. People saw it as a test for the AI regulation fights ahead.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Any consumer tech news worth knowing?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Google is opening the Play Store to outside payments.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Meaning?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: The thirty percent app store cut is going away.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: So you might pay developers directly for Android apps.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What's driving it?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It comes out of Google's long court fight with Epic.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: They're back in court on July sixteenth.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So this could still change?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It could. But the direction is clearly toward more open payments.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I thought the U.S. and Iran were de-escalating?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: They were. But overnight, the two sides exchanged strikes again.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Iran's Revolutionary Guard says it launched drones and missiles at Bahrain and Kuwait.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That's a serious step up.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It is — the biggest escalation since the recent ceasefire.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Is anyone trying to cool it down?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes. Iran's parliament speaker called an urgent meeting of a new conflict-control group with the U.S.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So where does that leave the ceasefire?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Shakier. This puts the talks for a lasting truce in real jeopardy.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: There's a difficult crime story today.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: An Australian man has been charged with murder in Thailand.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: A seventeen-year-old girl was found dead in a suitcase in Pattaya.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: How was he caught?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: He was arrested at Bangkok's airport after the girl went missing.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Police then found her body near railway tracks and identified her.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What are the charges?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Three — murder, concealing the body, and abducting a minor.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: A heavy case.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes. It's now moving through the Thai courts.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Any shift in the Russia–Ukraine war?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Putin admitted Russia has, in his words, a certain shortage of fuel.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It follows repeated Ukrainian strikes on Russian energy sites.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Is that working for Ukraine?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Kyiv calls it fair payback for years of attacks on its own infrastructure.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Russia now says its priorities are air defense and fuel, especially for Crimea.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Crimea specifically?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yeah — it declared an emergency over fuel shortages and power cuts.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So the strikes are really biting.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: They are. One recent hit set a refinery near Moscow on fire.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Something lighter to end on?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Sort of. There's a piece on a strange Jim Henson film called The Cube.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Henson — like the Muppets?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Same creator, but no Muppets here. It's eerie and grown-up.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: A man wakes in a plain white cube, with no idea how he got there.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: And then?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Things slowly unravel — his grip on reality, and what the cube even is.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Sounds like early Black Mirror.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Exactly the comparison people are making. A quiet, mind-bending oddity.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What's new in AI?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: A Chinese lab, Z.ai, released an open model called GLM-5.2.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Researchers say it now rivals top systems at finding security bugs.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Across the board?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Not quite — it still trails the leading U.S. models on general tasks.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: But the gap on cybersecurity has clearly narrowed.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Why does that matter?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: The U.S. has been restricting China's access to the most powerful models and chips.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: And this suggests the lead is shrinking.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Right — which is exactly what worries regulators.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What's the big US story today?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: The Supreme Court ruled against Trump's order to end birthright citizenship.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So people born in the U.S. still get citizenship?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes. The court said his order ran counter to the Constitution.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It was a six-to-three decision — a major blow to his immigration plans.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What did the order try to do?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Deny citizenship to babies born to undocumented parents or those on temporary status.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: The opinion said the administration's legal case simply wasn't strong enough.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So it's settled for now?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: For now, yes — the longstanding rule stands.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: There's an unusual crime story out of Monaco.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: A Ukrainian-born tycoon and his family were injured by a bomb.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: In Monaco? That's rare there.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Very. Officials called it an unprecedented attack on the principality.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: A man left a package in an apartment lobby, then walked away.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Are they treating it as terrorism?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Not yet. Prosecutors opened an attempted-murder investigation.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: And the search?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: An international manhunt is under way for the suspect.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Another Supreme Court ruling today?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes — it struck down limits on political party campaign spending.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Meaning more money in elections?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Right. One of the last caps on party spending is now gone.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: The court treats campaign spending as protected free speech.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Is this a new direction?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Not really — it follows Citizens United and years of loosening limits.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Those rulings gave rise to Super PACs spending without limit.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So today extends that trend.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Exactly — fewer guardrails on money in politics.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What's happening in games?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: IO Interactive, makers of 007 First Light, is laying off staff.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Even after a hit?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yeah — the game sold three million copies since May.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: They say they're committed to supporting affected employees.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What about future projects?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: They insist their next franchise, Project Fantasy, will continue.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Though they did lose an external partner on it.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So cuts despite success.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: A familiar story in the industry right now.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Any labor news in tech?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Rockstar workers are pushing to unionize before GTA Six launches.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: The Grand Theft Auto studio?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes. They've asked for their union to be voluntarily recognized.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It follows Rockstar firing over thirty staff last year.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Was that controversial?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Very — critics called it union busting.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: A tribunal over those firings starts in September.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Right before the game's launch?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes — GTA Six is planned for November nineteenth.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I saw Amnesty International released a report about Sudan. What happened?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: They've alleged that Sudan's paramilitary group, the Rapid Support Forces, committed crimes against humanity during their campaign to capture the city of El Fasher.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Where is El Fasher exactly?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It's in North Darfur state. It was the last stronghold held by Sudan's armed forces in the Darfur region before the RSF captured it in October after an eighteen-month siege.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What specifically is Amnesty accusing them of?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: The report alleges ethnic cleansing and that the RSF deliberately targeted children during attacks in the city. A UN fact-finding mission said earlier this year the seizure showed hallmarks of genocide against non-Arab communities.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: This sounds like it was unfolding for a long time.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes. Amnesty noted the world was warned about the horrors civilians faced as the siege continued. The eighteen months gave a long window to see what was happening.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I saw something tragic happened in Mexico City during World Cup celebrations?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes. Four people died as thousands of fans crowded the streets celebrating Mexico's victory over Ecuador. Three of those deaths were from suffocation.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Where exactly did this happen?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Near the Angel of Independence landmark. The city had closed the main boulevard and surrounding streets for the celebrations.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: How did it get so dangerous?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Thousands of people gathered in a concentrated area. The health secretariat reported the deaths in the early hours of Wednesday morning.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What's the city saying about this?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: The mayor, Clara Brugada, expressed condolences to the families and urged people to always celebrate with responsibility, care, and empathy.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That's heartbreaking during what should have been a joyful moment.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It is. Social media showed the contrast—fans cheering and chanting, while emergency responders were tending to distressed people on nearby streets.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I saw something about a guilty verdict in Dublin. What happened?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: A man named Riad Bouchaker was found guilty of attempting to murder three children in a stabbing attack in Dublin in November 2023.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That's awful. Where did this happen?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It was in Parnell Square in the city center. The children were coming out of a creche when Bouchaker attacked them with a 12-inch kitchen knife.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: How are the children?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: One girl, who was five at the time, was stabbed in the heart. The court also found him guilty of assault causing serious harm to a childcare worker named Leanne Flynn, and assaulting two other children and a teenager.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I remember this triggered something bigger in the city?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes. The attack horrified Ireland and triggered a riot in Dublin. The verdict came from a jury at the central criminal court on Wednesday.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So Comcast is splitting from NBCUniversal? What does that mean for Peacock?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It means Peacock will have to prove it can survive on its own, without being bundled with Comcast's other services.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Wait, it was bundled before?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes. Since its launch in 2020, Peacock was often treated as an accessory to an Xfinity subscription—kind of a bonus.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: But it has sports, right? Like the Olympics?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It does. Peacock streams the Olympics, Sunday Night Football, and Big Ten games. But even with that, it still trails behind rival streamers.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Why is it struggling compared to the others?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Part of it is availability. Unlike other major streamers, Peacock is only available in the US.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So this split is really a test for whether it can stand on its own.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Exactly. NBCUniversal executives are about to find out whether Peacock will sink or swim.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So Google just released a new smart speaker?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes, it's called the Google Home Speaker. It's their first new smart speaker in six years.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Six years? That's a long time. What makes this one different?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It's the first one built specifically for Gemini, which is Google's AI assistant. The company seems to be taking the smart home more seriously again.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: How does it compare to their older speaker?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Well, when compared side by side with the Nest Audio, which was their previous speaker at the same price, this new one doesn't sound as good.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That seems like a step backward. Is the Gemini assistant at least better?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: That's the catch. The headline suggests Gemini isn't quite ready for it yet. Google built good hardware, but the AI side still needs work.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So they're trying to catch up with Amazon?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Exactly. Amazon launched new hardware with a revamped Alexa last fall. Smart speakers have been looking for their next big purpose, and both companies are betting on better AI.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I'm seeing reports about a massive funeral procession in Iran. What happened?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Iran's supreme leader, Ali Khamenei, was assassinated. On Monday, millions of people gathered in Tehran for his funeral procession.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Millions? That's an enormous turnout.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes. Mourners wore black, carried flags with the slogan 'We will rise,' and held pictures of Khamenei. The Tehran metro was packed with people trying to join the march.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Do we know how he died?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: The details aren't fully clear from this report. Some are connecting it to broader tensions—many say this reflects a war launched on Iran by Donald Trump back in February.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: And Iranian officials have said anything about his absence before this?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: They did. Officials had said Khamenei's absence wasn't due to wounds from an Israeli attack on the presidential building, but rather concerns for his safety.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So there was already an attack before the assassination.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It appears so. Israel had struck the presidential building, but Iranian officials maintained Khamenei wasn't injured in that incident.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I saw something about a Syrian official convicted in Austria. What happened?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: An Austrian court found a former Syrian intelligence chief guilty of torture and sexual abuse. He worked in the city of Raqqa under Bashar al-Assad's government.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So this trial happened in Vienna, not Syria?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes, in Vienna. The man is identified only as Khaled al-H. He's from the Druze minority.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Was he the only one on trial?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: No. A second Syrian official was also convicted—the former police chief in Raqqa. Both were found guilty of abusing political opponents.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Why does this matter now?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It's part of ongoing efforts to hold former Syrian officials accountable for crimes committed during the conflict, even years later and in other countries.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So China just tested a missile in the South Pacific?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes. A Chinese navy statement said a nuclear submarine launched what they called a strategic missile with a training warhead on Monday. They say it landed accurately in a designated sea area.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: How are other countries reacting?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Australia's foreign minister, Penny Wong, called it destabilising to the region. New Zealand's Winston Peters described it as deeply concerning.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Is the timing significant here?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It does stand out. The test happened just hours after Australia and Fiji signed a major defence agreement, where each country commits to help the other if attacked.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Do we know what kind of missile it was?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: China's defence ministry didn't confirm whether it was an intercontinental ballistic missile. They just said it was a strategic missile with a training simulation warhead.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Why does this matter for the region?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It's seen as a show of military capability in an area where tensions are already high, especially with new defence partnerships forming.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So there's a whole agency business now for online creators?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes. UTA, one of the big talent agencies, represents creators just like they represent traditional actors or authors.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What does that actually look like day to day?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: They're helping creators launch their own products now, not just make content. It's a shift from making media to building brands.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That's interesting. Are creators worried about anything right now?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: AI and platform changes seem to be the big concerns. Both could really shake up how creators work and earn.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: How did this agent get into representing creators?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: They started at another agency, then moved to UTA and worked on the traditional literary side before getting into the creator space.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So this is all becoming a real industry.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Exactly. It's big business now, with the same kind of representation you'd see in film or publishing.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So Nintendo's stopping sales of the original Switch in Europe?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes, starting mid-February 2027. That includes the standard Switch, the Lite, and the OLED model.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That's almost ten years after it first launched. Why now?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It ties to new European battery regulations. Nintendo's updating the Switch 2 to have a replaceable battery there.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Just the Switch 2, or other things too?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Controllers as well — Joy-Con 2, the Pro Controller, even the N64 and GameCube controllers will get user-replaceable batteries.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: When does that updated Switch 2 arrive?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Expected to start rolling out in the fall.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So Europe gets a different version than the rest of the world?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: For now, yes — at least to meet those specific battery rules.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I saw something about a Syrian official being convicted in Austria. What happened?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: A former Syrian intelligence chief from the city of Raqqa was found guilty by a court in Vienna. He was convicted of torture and sexual abuse of people who opposed Bashar al-Assad.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Wait, how did an Austrian court end up trying someone for crimes in Syria?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: That's a good question. Some countries have laws allowing them to prosecute serious human rights crimes regardless of where they happened. Austria appears to be using that authority here.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Was he the only one on trial?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: No. A second Syrian official was also found guilty—the former police chief in Raqqa. He was convicted of abusing political opponents as well.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Do we know anything about the intelligence chief?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: He's identified only as Khaled al-H. He's a member of the Druze ethno-religious minority and was convicted specifically of committing torture.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: This must be part of a broader effort to hold people accountable for what happened in Syria.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It does seem that way. Courts in Europe have been increasingly willing to pursue these cases as victims and former officials have ended up in their countries.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I'm seeing reports about a huge funeral procession in Iran. What happened?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Iran's supreme leader, Ali Khamenei, was assassinated. On Monday, a massive crowd—officials say millions—gathered in Tehran for his funeral procession.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Millions? That's enormous. What was the scene like?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Mourners wore black and carried flags with the slogan 'We will rise.' Many also held Iranian flags and pictures of Khamenei. The Tehran metro was packed with people trying to join the march.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: How is this being interpreted politically?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Some observers are calling the turnout a response to what they see as a misguided war launched on Iran by Donald Trump back in February.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Do we know more about how Khamenei died?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Iranian officials say his absence before this wasn't due to wounds from an Israeli attack on the presidential building, but rather concerns for his safety. The details around his assassination are still emerging.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: This feels like a major turning point for the region.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It is. Iran is now without its supreme leader, and the scale of this procession shows just how significant and uncertain this moment is.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I saw something about Mohamed Salah and Egypt's football history. What's going on there?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It's an interesting tension. Salah led Egypt to two World Cups — 2018 and 2026 — which is a big deal. But for some Egyptian fans, that doesn't quite make up for something from the past.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What are they still upset about?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: There was a generation of players, called the 'golden generation,' who never made it to the World Cups in 2006, 2010, or 2014. For those fans, that's a lasting disappointment.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So even though Salah got them there, it's not enough?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Right. And it's complicated by the fact that Egypt was eliminated first in 2018. Plus, the national team hasn't won the Africa Cup of Nations since 2010, and Salah's shouldered a lot of blame for that drought.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That seems harsh, given he's one of the best players in the Premier League.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It does. But that's the weight of expectation. He's the captain and leader now, so frustrations tend to land on him, fairly or not.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So there's this whole world of agents representing online creators now?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes. UTA, one of the big talent agencies, is deeply involved in the creator economy — representing people who built their careers online.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What does that actually look like? What are these agents doing?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: They're helping creators launch their own products. So it's not just about making videos or podcasts anymore — these stars are creating physical goods and brands.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That's a big shift from just making content.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Exactly. And the conversation also touched on AI and social media platforms — both seen as potentially destabilizing forces for creators right now.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Destabilizing how?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: The piece doesn't spell it out, but those technologies are changing how content gets made and distributed, which affects how creators build careers and earn money.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: And this story came from someone who actually worked at UTA?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Right. They started at another agency, then moved to UTA and worked on the traditional literary side before getting into this world.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So Nintendo's stopping sales of the original Switch in Europe?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes, starting mid-February next year. That includes the regular Switch, the Lite, and the OLED model.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That's almost ten years since it first launched, right?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Exactly. The Switch came out in March 2017, so it'll be just shy of a decade.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Why now though? Is this just about the Switch 2?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Partly. There's a bigger reason — new European battery regulations. Nintendo's making a version of the Switch 2 with a replaceable battery to comply.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Oh, so users can swap the battery themselves?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Right. That updated Switch 2 is expected to roll out in the fall. They're also updating Joy-Con controllers and some other accessories the same way.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: And the original Switch just doesn't meet those rules?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It seems Nintendo decided it's simpler to phase it out rather than redesign older hardware. The focus is clearly on the new generation now.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So the ceasefire between the US and Iran... it sounds like it's falling apart?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It does. Both sides are accusing each other of breaking the agreement that was signed on June 17th.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What actually happened?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: The US launched strikes around the strait of Hormuz and also revoked a temporary waiver that let Iran export oil. Iran then hit back at US bases in Bahrain and Kuwait.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: And Trump thinks the ceasefire is over?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: He said he thought it was over. He also said the US attacked what he called very dangerous people affiliated with Iran.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Why does this matter so much?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It shows how fragile the agreement is. The strikes and the decision to cancel the oil waiver have brought things right to the edge of unraveling completely.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I'm seeing news about a cargo plane that's gone missing off Pakistan's coast?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes. A Boeing 737 cargo plane operated by K2 Airways disappeared after flying from Sharjah in the United Arab Emirates. Search efforts are now under way.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Do they know what happened to it?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Flight tracking data shows the plane went through some very unusual altitude changes — it dropped about five thousand feet in less than a minute, then climbed back six thousand feet in thirty seconds, before a steep final descent from over thirty-six thousand feet.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That sounds alarming. Where did this happen?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Southwest of Karachi. The tracking data suggests it may have gone down into the sea there.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: How old was this plane?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Twenty-seven years old. It's part of Boeing's older 737 family — two generations older than the MAX version that's been in the news for safety issues.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What's K2 Airways saying?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: They've said they're cooperating with Pakistan's Civil Aviation Authority and other government agencies. Boeing hasn't commented yet.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So the World Cup quarterfinals are all set?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes. Eight teams made it through—France, Morocco, Norway, England, Spain, Belgium, Argentina, and Switzerland. The matches will be played across the United States.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Sounds exciting. But I'm hearing there's some controversy too?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: There is. Egypt has alleged officiating bias after losing to Argentina. They're disputing how the match was handled.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: And there's something about the US team and President Trump?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Right. FIFA rescinded a red-card suspension for a player named Balogun after Trump publicly requested it. That decision has drawn a lot of scrutiny.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So people are worried about officiating integrity overall?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Exactly. These incidents have raised broader concerns about fairness and how decisions are being made at this tournament.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I saw Meta's doing something new with their smart glasses?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes. They're rolling out an update that will disable the camera if someone tampers with the privacy light.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: The privacy light — that's the little LED that shows when you're recording?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Exactly. It lets people nearby know the camera is on. But some users have been tampering with it, even physically drilling into it.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Why would someone do that?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Presumably to record without others knowing. Meta's tried to discourage this before, but now they're taking a harder line.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So if the light's been messed with, the camera just won't work?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Right. The glasses will detect the tampering and shut the camera off entirely.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I guess privacy concerns have been pretty serious with these devices.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: They have. Some public venues are even considering banning the glasses altogether because of those worries.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I saw ABC is pushing back against the FCC. What's this about?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: The FCC opened an investigation into The View — specifically whether it should still count as a real news program.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Why does that classification matter?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: News programs are exempt from the equal time rule, which says if you give one political candidate airtime, you have to offer the same to their opponents.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So what triggered the investigation?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: The View interviewed Representative James Talarico, a Democrat from Texas who's running for Senate. The FCC Chair, Brendan Carr, is now reexamining whether The View qualifies for that news exemption.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: And ABC isn't happy about this.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Right. In a letter to the FCC, ABC argues the agency is targeting programs it sees as unfriendly to the current administration — and that this threatens editorial independence.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So it's really about who gets to decide what counts as news.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Exactly. ABC is telling the government to stay out of its newsrooms.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So the ceasefire between the US and Iran—it's still holding, right?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It's under serious strain. Overnight Thursday, the US launched airstrikes in Iran that killed at least fourteen people. Iran then responded by striking targets in Gulf countries.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That sounds like more than just a strain. Are we talking about a full breakdown?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Not yet, but these were the most intense strikes since the ceasefire was extended last month. The president posted videos of the explosions and issued new threats afterward.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Wait—he'd said something different earlier, hadn't he?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes. Hours before the strikes, he promised they'd be very fast and wouldn't lead to long-term fighting.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What are they actually disagreeing about at this point?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Significant gaps remain—mainly over Iran's control of the Strait of Hormuz and inspections of nuclear facilities. Those issues haven't been resolved in negotiations.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So the ceasefire agreement from June is still technically in place?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Technically, yes. That memorandum was meant to give space for talks toward a permanent truce, but Thursday's strikes show how fragile that space really is.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I saw Graham Platner suspended his Senate campaign in Maine. What happened?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: A woman accused him of sexual assault. In the days after that, Democrats called on him to drop out of the race.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: This was considered an important race, right?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes. Democrats see it as pivotal to their chances of gaining control of the Senate. He's running against Republican Susan Collins.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Did he say why he's suspending now?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: In a video, he said it's not an admission of guilt, but that Democrats threatened to cut off his campaign support.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Is he officially out of the race?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Not yet. He says he'll withdraw once he's assured his replacement is selected in a transparent and democratic way.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Were there other issues before this?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes. In June, the New York Times reported he'd exchanged sexual messages with other women while married, and his wife had warned his campaign about it.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I heard there's some kind of problem with border checks in Greece?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes. The boss of 14 Greek airports is calling for an overhaul of the EU's new border checks. They've had to put up gazebos outside because passengers are queuing in the sun waiting to be processed.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Gazebos? That sounds pretty chaotic.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It is. They're also prioritizing vulnerable people through security to keep them safe. The airport boss described the situation as unpleasant and dangerous.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Is this just happening in Greece?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: No. An airline industry group reported delays and missed connections last week in Portugal, Spain, Italy, and Belgium. Ryanair warned of queue chaos at popular destinations like Alicante, Málaga, and Palma.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Are UK travelers affected by this?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: UK passengers make up most of the non-EU tourists at these Greek airports, which include Corfu, Rhodes, Mykonos, and Crete. Greek authorities have indicated police won't check UK passengers, though there's no official legal exemption from the biometric tests.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So the new system just isn't working smoothly yet.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: That's what it looks like. The calls for an overhaul suggest these aren't just temporary teething problems.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I saw Samsung is offering some kind of credit for their new phones. What's going on?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Samsung has new foldable Galaxy phones coming soon, and they're offering a thirty dollar store credit if you register your interest ahead of time.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So the phones haven't even been announced yet?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Right. They're expected to be updated versions of the Galaxy Z Fold phones, but Samsung is letting people sign up early.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: How does the credit actually work?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: You register now, then when the phones are officially announced, you'd preorder one and get the thirty dollar credit applied at checkout.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Do I have to use that credit right away?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes, you have to use it when you preorder the phone. It's not something you can save for later.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Is there any catch to registering?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: No downside. You're just filling out a form to show interest. So if you're even thinking about buying one, it's worth doing.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So Meta is working on smart glasses that record all the time?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes, they're developing prototype glasses they call 'super sensing.' According to the Financial Times, these would continuously record audio and snap photos every few seconds.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Every few seconds? What would happen to all that footage?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: The idea is you could ask Meta AI about what you've captured—like searching through your day by voice.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That sounds like a lot of data being sent to Meta's servers.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Actually, they say the full audio and images wouldn't be uploaded. Instead, metadata would be extracted and sent to the server for the AI to query.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: And that's supposed to be more private?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: That's what proponents argue—that using metadata instead of raw recordings would have fewer privacy implications.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Still, always-on recording feels like a big step.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It is. If this moves forward, Meta would be joining the race to make always-on AI wearables.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I saw there's been a terrible wildfire in Spain. What happened?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: A wildfire in southern Spain has killed at least eleven people. It's one of the deadliest fires the country has seen.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That's awful. Where exactly is this happening?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Near Los Gallardos, in the Almeria region of southern Spain. The area has been dealing with very high temperatures.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Are they able to fight it?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes. About 150 firefighters and 220 soldiers from Spain's military emergency unit are working to contain the blaze. Six others have been injured.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What's making these fires so bad?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Scientists say climate change is making heat and dryness worse in certain regions, which makes them more vulnerable to wildfires like this one.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So it sounds like things have calmed down between the U.S. and Iran?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes, after two days of intense strikes, the fighting appears to have paused.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Was there something specific happening in Iran during all this?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: There was. A weeklong funeral was underway for former Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and four of his family members, who were killed on the first day of the conflict.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That's a lot happening at once. What about Gaza—what's changing there?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Israel's zone of control inside Gaza is expanding, and people are living under those conditions now.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Is there any plan for what happens next in Gaza?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: There's a deal being discussed that calls for an Israeli withdrawal from Gaza, new governance there, and Hamas's disarmament.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So still a lot of uncertainty for people on the ground.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Exactly. The situation remains very difficult for those living there.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I saw the EU is going after Meta about addictive design. What's happening there?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: EU regulators are accusing Meta of failing to address how Facebook and Instagram's design affects users' physical and mental health, especially children.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What specifically are they concerned about?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: The commission says Meta ignored information about how much time kids spend on these apps at night, and how features like reels and stories can lead to excessive or even compulsive use.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Is this breaking any rules?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes. The EU says it violates the Digital Services Act, which is meant to protect users from various online harms.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What does Meta say about all this?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: A spokesperson said they disagree with the preliminary findings and believe the EU hasn't properly considered the steps they've taken to protect teens.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Why does this matter now?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It's part of an investigation that started in May last year, and it comes as the EU is actually considering a social media ban for minors.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I saw Microsoft's emissions went up quite a bit. What happened?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Their carbon emissions rose 25 percent last year. It's a setback for their goal to be carbon negative by 2030.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Carbon negative—that means removing more than they produce, right?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Exactly. They set that target several years ago, but they're moving in the wrong direction right now.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What's causing the increase?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Their report points to AI infrastructure driving up demand for energy, water, land, and materials. And sustainability solutions aren't scaling fast enough to keep up.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Is Microsoft the only tech company dealing with this?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: No. Google reported a 25 percent spike in supply chain emissions too. Amazon's increase was a bit lower, at 16 percent.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So this is part of a broader pattern with AI growth.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It seems that way. And for Microsoft, it's not the first time—their 2024 report showed a similar rise.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So OpenAI is shutting down that browser they just launched?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes, ChatGPT Atlas. It was a browser that could do tasks for you automatically, but it's being shut down less than a year after launch.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That's pretty quick. Is this part of a bigger pattern?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It seems so. In recent months, OpenAI has also shut down their video generation app Sora and paused plans for something called ChatGPT adult mode.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What's driving all these shutdowns?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: OpenAI is apparently trying to reduce what they call side quests and focus on catching up with a competitor, Anthropic, on productivity features.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So they're narrowing their focus.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Exactly. It looks like they're pulling back from some experiments to concentrate on core products.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So the Trump administration subpoenaed New York Times journalists?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes. It's over their reporting on Air Force One and security concerns about the plane.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What did the Times report exactly?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: They said Trump flew to a NATO summit in Turkey on a new plane that Qatar had gifted, but the Secret Service advised him to switch to an older plane for the trip home.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Why the switch?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: The reporting suggested the Qatari plane wasn't secure enough. A former official told CBS there hadn't been enough time or money to update it with the required security features.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: And the subpoenas are about the sources for that story?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: That seems to be the case. The Times used unnamed sources. The paper's top newsroom lawyer called the subpoenas a brazen attempt to intimidate journalists and prevent the public from knowing what's happening.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That's a pretty strong response.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It is. Press freedom advocates tend to view subpoenas of journalists as a serious issue, especially when reporting involves government accountability.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I saw something about a US congressman being detained in the West Bank. What happened?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Congressman Ro Khanna says he was detained by armed Israeli settlers during a recent visit to the occupied West Bank.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Detained by settlers? How did that happen?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: He says settlers carrying US-made M4 rifles surrounded their van. An aide who was there said Israeli forces showed up to back the settlers, not the congressman.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That's surprising. How did they get out of the situation?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: They contacted the US embassy and Israeli police, and were eventually able to continue traveling.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What did the congressman say about the experience?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: He described it as a first-hand view of the realities faced by Palestinians living under occupation.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I just saw that South African footballer Jayden Adams has died. He was so young.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes, he was only 25. He'd just played for South Africa at the World Cup a few weeks ago.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What happened?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Police in South Africa say they found a 25-year-old man's body at a house in Cape Town on Saturday morning. They've opened an investigation.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That's awful. How did he do at the World Cup?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: He played in all three group matches. South Africa actually made history by reaching the knockout stages for the first time, though they lost to Canada in the round of 32.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I remember reading something about him playing through difficult circumstances.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes. He started in their opening match against the Czech Republic just hours after learning his grandmother had passed away.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That must have taken incredible strength.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Wait, what's this about a surveillance company threatening people?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: So, photos of what looked like cease and desist letters from Flock Safety went viral. They appeared to demand a lecture series in Newport Beach stop discussing Flock's surveillance technology.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That sounds pretty aggressive. Did they actually send those letters?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: No. Flock says they didn't send them — that the letters didn't come from anyone at the company.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So someone faked them?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: The company is saying the letters aren't real, though we don't know yet who created them or why.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Why would this blow up so much?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Flock has already faced significant backlash over its surveillance technology and work with law enforcement. So when these letters appeared, it triggered another wave of criticism.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Even though they say they didn't send them.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Right. The fake letters still fed into existing concerns people have about the company.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I saw something about a synth called Nopia that's been in the works for a while. What's the story there?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: After years of teasing, the creators say Nopia is basically finished. They recently brought it in for a first look and announced it'll launch in a couple of months for around £550.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So what makes this synth different from others?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It's built around harmonic interplay in a unique way. Instead of just a few knobs and a keyboard controlling one synth patch, it blends multiple modules — keys, bass, arp, and pad — into a single performance.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That sounds pretty different. How do you actually play it?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: There's a one-octave keyboard called the Chord Builder, a 12-button Tonal Selector, and an Extensions Dial. Together, these control the key and voicing of the chords you're creating.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So it's more like building a whole arrangement than just playing one sound?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Exactly. They compare it to a drumless groovebox — you're layering and shaping multiple parts at once rather than focusing on a single melody line.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I just saw that Senator Lindsey Graham has died. What happened?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: His office announced on Sunday that he died from a sudden illness. There had been no known health concerns before this.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That's shocking. When did this happen?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: According to the Washington Post, emergency services received a call Saturday evening around eight-thirty about someone with chest pains at his home on Capitol Hill.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Wasn't he just active recently?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes. His birthday was last Thursday, and he visited Ukraine last week. He was even scheduled to appear on Meet the Press on Sunday.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: He was close to Donald Trump, wasn't he?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Very close. He was a key political ally and frequent golf partner. His office described this as a personal blow to Trump.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What was he doing in Ukraine?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: President Zelenskyy thanked him on Friday for recognizing Ukrainian warriors. Graham was a longtime senator and had been involved in various international issues.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I'm seeing reports about new strikes between the US and Iran. What happened?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Early Sunday, Iran's Revolutionary Guard said it struck a container ship that was using what they called an unauthorised route through the Strait of Hormuz. Then US Central Command responded with strikes against at least 140 targets in Iran.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: And Iran closed the strait again?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes. The Revolutionary Guard said the strait is closed until further notice — at least until what they called the end of US interference in the region. They warned any retaliation would bring a severe response.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Why does this waterway matter so much?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: The Strait of Hormuz is a vital shipping route. That's why the Australian government is urging Iran to uphold the ceasefire agreement and allow safe passage through it.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Does this mean talks about the waterway are in trouble?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It raises doubts, yes. There were hopes for talks to agree on a way forward, but this latest flare-up makes that harder.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I'm seeing news from Qatar this morning. What happened?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: The Qatari government announced that former Emir Sheikh Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani has died.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Former emir... so he wasn't currently leading the country?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Right. He ruled Qatar from 1995 until 2013, when he stepped down.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What's he known for?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: He's widely regarded as one of the key architects of modern Qatar.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Have there been reactions from other leaders?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes. Egyptian President Abdel Fattah el-Sisi expressed his deepest condolences to Qatar's leaders and people.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: It sounds like he had a significant impact on the region.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: He did. The statements from leaders emphasize prayers for mercy and wishes for Qatar's continued stability.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I saw something about a surveillance company and fake legal letters going around?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Right. So photos of what looked like cease and desist letters from Flock Safety went viral this week.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What did the letters say?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: They demanded that a lecture series in Newport Beach stop hosting conversations about Flock's surveillance technology.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That sounds pretty aggressive. Did Flock actually send them?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: No. Flock says the letters didn't come from them or anyone at the company.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So someone faked legal threats in their name?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It appears so. But the damage was done — Flock already faces criticism over its work with law enforcement, and this triggered another wave of backlash.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Even though they didn't actually send it.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Exactly. The company is now dealing with fallout from something they say they never did.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So what's this Nopia synth I keep hearing about?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It's a synthesizer that's been teased for years and is now basically finished. The creators say it'll launch in a couple of months for around £550.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What makes it different from other synths?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It's built around harmonic interplay in a unique way. Instead of just controlling one synth patch, it blends multiple modules — keys, bass, arp, and pad — into a single performance.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Like playing several instruments at once?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Exactly. They compare it to a drumless groovebox. You've got a one-octave keyboard called the Chord Builder, a 12-button Tonal Selector, and an Extensions Dial that all work together to shape the key and voicing of your chords.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That sounds pretty hands-on.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It is. The creators recently brought it to MusicRadar's offices for an in-depth first look, so people are finally getting to see how it actually works.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So Iran launched more strikes overnight?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes. Iran's Revolutionary Guard says it targeted US military facilities in Bahrain, and also hit sites in Oman, Jordan, and Kuwait.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What exactly did they hit in Bahrain?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: They claim they launched missiles and drones at US military installations in an area called Juffair. Bahrain's government said explosions people heard were from air defense systems intercepting the attacks.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: And this is all in response to US strikes?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Right. The US military had just hit dozens of Iranian targets, saying they wanted to stop Iran from attacking ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So both sides are escalating?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: That's the concern. These latest Iranian strikes came as a direct response to Washington's attacks, and prospects for peace between the two countries are fading.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I saw that Qatar's former leader passed away. Sheikh Hamad, I think?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes, Sheikh Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani. He died at seventy-four. He ruled Qatar for eighteen years.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What did he do during that time?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: He really transformed the country's economy. When he came to power in 1995, Qatar was small and relied mainly on oil.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: And he changed that?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: He did. Qatar had this huge natural gas field that wasn't fully developed yet. Under his leadership, the country became the world's largest exporter of liquefied natural gas.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That's a big shift.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It was. In less than twenty years, Qatar also built one of the largest sovereign wealth funds and became one of the highest per capita income countries in the world.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So he turned Qatar into a major energy player.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Exactly. From a small Gulf economy to a major force in global energy and investment markets.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I saw counter-terrorism police have taken over the Ann Widdecombe case. What changed?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Initially, they reviewed it and ruled out a terrorist motive. But now they say new information and evidence came to light during the investigation.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So they've changed their assessment?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes. Counter-terrorism policing south east is now leading what they're calling a dynamic and complex investigation.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What about the person in custody?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: A 28-year-old man from Rotherham was arrested on suspicion of murder on Saturday. He's since been rearrested on suspicion of terrorism-related offences.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That's quite a shift in the investigation.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It is. The head of national counter-terrorism policing said they're building on the earlier work and following where the new evidence leads.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So Philips Hue... that's the smart light bulb system, right?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Exactly. And apparently it's one of the few smart home products that's actually gotten things right.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What does that mean, getting it right?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Well, the idea is that making your home smart shouldn't require tearing down walls or renovating. The technology should just blend in.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: And Hue does that?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: According to this piece, it comes closer than maybe any other product so far. You just screw in a bulb.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: How did it get started?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Interestingly, its origins are tied to the rise of smartphones. The Verge brought in a smart home expert to talk about how it all came together.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So smartphones made smart lights possible?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Pretty much. Once everyone had a powerful computer in their pocket, controlling your lights from an app started to make sense.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So Apple tried to make a self-driving car?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: They did, yeah. The program never really got off the ground, but there's an interesting side effect.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What came out of it?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Early on, Apple realized they'd need really powerful chips to handle AI processing right on the device. That pushed their chip development forward.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So the car project actually helped their other products?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Exactly. While their AI software has lagged behind competitors, the hardware itself has been impressive. Those chips are now in their computers and phones.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Does that affect how we use Apple devices?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It does. Because the chips can handle more on the device itself, less of your data needs to be sent to the cloud. That's become a big privacy selling point for Apple.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Interesting how a failed project can still leave something valuable behind.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Right. And they're now planning a new server product based on that chip technology, with support for up to one and a half terabytes of memory.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So the US has been striking Iran for three nights now?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes, this is the third consecutive night. And it's happening even though the US and Iran are supposed to be in a 60-day interim deal that was meant to lead to talks about ending the war.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Wait, there's a war? When did that start?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It began in February with the assassination of Iran's supreme leader, Ali Khamenei. The current deal was supposed to set up a path toward peace, but it's falling apart.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: And now Trump is talking about charging ships?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Right. He's threatening to reinstate a maritime blockade and charge ships for safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz. It's a major shift in his position on tolls.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That strait keeps coming up in the news. What happened there?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Two UAE tankers were hit by Iranian cruise missiles in the southern lane, in Omani waters. One Indian crew member was killed and eight others were wounded, four of them seriously.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So the ceasefire isn't really holding.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: No. There's been a series of attacks over the strait, and the interim ceasefire has nearly collapsed. World leaders are worried the conflict could fully resume.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I saw something about a terrible fire in Bangkok. What happened?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: A pub fire early Monday morning has now killed thirty people. It's the deadliest blaze the city has seen in seventeen years.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That's awful. Do they know what caused it?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Disaster officials believe an electrical short circuit in an air conditioner in the ceiling started the fire.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: And police are investigating negligence?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes. The national police chief said negligence is the primary theory they're working with right now.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What kind of negligence are they looking at?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: One concern is that an exit near the kitchen may have been narrowed by shelving units and lockers, which could have made escape harder.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Is the pub cooperating?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: They've said they will cooperate with the investigation.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I saw China hit a big milestone with car exports. What happened?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: For the first time ever, China exported more than one million cars in a single month — that was in June. Overall exports from China jumped 27 percent.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Where are all these cars going?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: A lot are heading to the EU. Exports there rose nearly 13 percent compared to last year, and China's trade surplus with Europe reached about 135 billion pounds.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Are we talking about regular cars or electric ones?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Many are electric vehicles and hybrids. Interestingly, these avoided EU tariffs that were put on Chinese EVs in 2024, so they've been flowing in steadily.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That must be affecting European carmakers.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It is. The European auto industry is under serious pressure, with recent warnings about potential job losses in the sector.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: And this fits into China's bigger trade picture?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Exactly. China's on track to match or even beat last year's record trade surplus of one trillion dollars, despite ongoing trade tensions.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So there's a company that wants to bounce sunlight back to Earth at night?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes. Reflect Orbital just got approval to launch their first space mirror. It's called Eärendil-1, and it has an 18-meter reflective surface designed to redirect sunlight to specific spots on the ground after dark.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: How much light are we talking about?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: If it works, they say it could illuminate areas up to 5 kilometers wide. And this is just the first one — they're planning a constellation of 50,000 satellites by 2035.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That's a lot of satellites. Are people worried about that?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: They are. Astronomers have already raised concerns that these mirrors could interfere with observations. And more broadly, there's growing worry about space junk and light pollution from all these satellite constellations.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So it's not just about whether it works, but whether we should do it.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Exactly. The technology might be possible, but the environmental and scientific trade-offs are real.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So Apple just dropped some new software betas?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes, public betas for iOS 27 and several other operating systems are available now.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What's the main draw here?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: You can try out the new Siri before the wider launch this fall.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What other systems got betas?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: iPadOS 27, watchOS 27, and macOS 27 Golden Gate are all available too.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: How do I actually get it on my phone?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Look for a section called Beta Updates at the top of your screen, tap that, then select the iOS 27 public beta.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: And it's similar for the other devices?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Right, the setup steps are generally the same across Apple products.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I saw there was a terrible fire in Bangkok. What happened?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: A fire broke out early Monday morning at a pub in Bangkok. The death toll has now reached 30, making it the city's deadliest blaze in 17 years.

captions.speaker: listener
